tasks.title: Initialize Cargo Project and Dependencies
tasks.description: Set up the Rust project workspace with Cargo, including all required dependencies as specified in the PRD.
tasks.details: Create a new Cargo project named 'sleek-markdown-editor'. Add dependencies in Cargo.toml: egui 0.28, eframe 0.28, comrak 0.22, syntect 5.1, serde with derive, serde_json 1, dirs 5, notify 6, open 5, rfd 0.14. Verify that the project builds successfully with `cargo build`.
tasks.testStrategy: Run `cargo build` to ensure all dependencies are correctly resolved and the project compiles without errors.

subtasks.title: Create New Cargo Project 'sleek-markdown-editor'
subtasks.description: Initialize a new Rust project workspace named 'sleek-markdown-editor' using Cargo with the default edition and binary setup.

subtasks.testStrategy: Verify the project directory and files exist. Run `cargo build` to ensure the initial project builds successfully.

subtasks.title: Add and Verify Dependencies in Cargo.toml

subtasks.details: Edit the Cargo.toml file in 'sleek-markdown-editor' to include dependencies: egui 0.28, eframe 0.28, comrak 0.22, syntect 5.1, serde with derive feature, serde_json 1, dirs 5, notify 6, open 5, and rfd 0.14. Run `cargo build` to confirm all dependencies are downloaded and the project compiles without errors.
subtasks.testStrategy: Run `cargo build` and ensure no compilation errors occur. Confirm dependencies are listed in Cargo.lock and Cargo.toml with correct versions.

tasks.title: Implement Error Handling Module
tasks.description: Create a centralized error handling system with custom Result types and error enums covering file, config, and parse errors.
tasks.details: Define an error enum in error.rs covering file I/O errors, configuration errors, and markdown parsing errors. Implement Display and Error traits for user-friendly messages. Provide a custom Result type alias for convenience. Include error logging and graceful degradation strategies.
tasks.testStrategy: Write unit tests to verify error creation, formatting, and propagation. Test error display for various error cases.

subtasks.title: Define Centralized Error Enum in error.rs
subtasks.description: Create a comprehensive error enum covering file I/O, configuration, and markdown parsing errors to centralize error representation.
subtasks.details: In error.rs, define an enum that includes variants for file I/O errors, configuration errors, and markdown parsing errors. Use Rust's standard error types where appropriate and wrap them to unify error handling across modules.
subtasks.testStrategy: Write unit tests to verify that each error variant can be created and matches expected error cases.

subtasks.title: Implement Display and Error Traits for User-Friendly Messages
subtasks.description: Implement the std::fmt::Display and std::error::Error traits for the error enum to provide clear, user-friendly error messages.
subtasks.details: For the error enum defined in subtask 1, implement the Display trait to format error messages clearly for end users. Also implement the Error trait to integrate with Rust's error handling ecosystem, enabling error chaining and compatibility.
subtasks.testStrategy: Test that error messages are formatted correctly and that the error trait methods behave as expected.

subtasks.title: Add Custom Result Type Alias, Logging, and Graceful Degradation
subtasks.description: Create a custom Result type alias for convenience, integrate error logging, and implement graceful degradation strategies for error handling.
subtasks.details: Define a type alias for Result<T, Error> to simplify function signatures. Integrate logging (e.g., using the log crate) to record errors centrally. Implement strategies to handle errors gracefully, such as fallback defaults or recovery paths, to maintain application stability.
subtasks.testStrategy: Verify that errors are logged appropriately and that fallback mechanisms activate correctly under error conditions.

tasks.expansionPrompt: Define error enums, implement traits for user-friendly messages, and add logging and graceful degradation.

subtasks.title: Add Robust Error Handling and Fallback Mechanisms for Config Persistence
subtasks.description: Handle cases of missing or corrupted config files gracefully by falling back to default configurations and providing error handling.

tasks.title: Create WYSIWYG Markdown Editor Widget
tasks.description: Build a widget that renders markdown as editable egui widgets, allowing users to edit content directly in rendered view.
tasks.details:
In markdown/editor.rs, create a `MarkdownEditor` widget that provides a WYSIWYG markdown editing experience in a single editing area with toggleable raw and rendered modes. The widget should:
1. Take markdown source text as input.
2. Parse the markdown into an AST using the `comrak` crate.
3. Render each AST node as an editable egui widget (headings, paragraphs, lists, code blocks, etc.), not read-only.
4. Propagate user edits in the rendered widgets back into the underlying markdown representation, updating the AST and source text as needed.
5. Return the modified markdown text after user interaction, suitable for storing in `AppState`.
6. Support switching between raw markdown text editing and rendered WYSIWYG editing, without any separate preview pane, scroll synchronization, or split view concepts.
tasks.testStrategy: Create test markdown documents containing headings, paragraphs, lists, and code blocks. In rendered (WYSIWYG) mode, edit the content through the egui widgets and verify that the underlying markdown source text returned by `MarkdownEditor` updates correctly and preserves markdown structure where possible.

tasks.title: Implement Editable Link and Inline Formatting Widgets
tasks.description: Create widgets for editing links and inline formatting (bold, italic) in WYSIWYG rendered mode.
tasks.details:
In markdown/elements.rs, implement WYSIWYG editing widgets for inline markdown elements:
1. Implement an **EditableLink** widget so that clicking on a rendered markdown link enters an inline edit mode for BOTH the link text and the URL. Edits must be reflected back into the underlying markdown as `[text](url)` syntax, updating both label and destination while preserving surrounding content.
2. Implement inline **bold** and **italic** editing where styled text remains directly editable in-place. Typing within bold/italic regions must preserve the surrounding `**bold**` and `*italic*` delimiters and update the markdown source accordingly.
3. Add keyboard shortcuts **Ctrl+B** for bold and **Ctrl+I** for italic when there is a text selection. Applying a shortcut should wrap the selection in the corresponding markdown delimiters (or remove them when toggling off), updating both the rendered WYSIWYG view and the underlying markdown text.

Users should be able to:
- Click a rendered link to edit its text and destination URL, not just open it.
- Select text and apply or remove bold/italic formatting using toolbar actions or keyboard shortcuts.
- Continue editing within formatted regions while markdown syntax is preserved and kept in sync with the rendered view.
tasks.testStrategy:
In WYSIWYG rendered mode, create and edit links and apply bold/italic formatting:
- Create a new link, edit its text and URL inline, and verify the markdown source updates to the correct `[text](url)` form.
- Modify existing links and confirm both label and destination changes are reflected in the markdown.
- Select text and apply bold/italic via Ctrl+B / Ctrl+I and/or UI controls; verify the markdown inserts/removes the appropriate `**` or `*` delimiters around the selection.
- Edit text inside bold/italic regions and confirm the styling remains correct and the delimiters stay properly balanced in the markdown source.

tasks.title: Dead Code Cleanup
tasks.description: Remove all identified unused code paths, data structures, and helper utilities across the codebase and update public-facing documentation so that `cargo check` runs with zero warnings after cleanup.
tasks.details:
Implementation steps:

1. **Preparation and safety mechanisms**
- Ensure the current `main` branch builds and tests cleanly with `cargo check` and the existing test suite.
- Temporarily enable `#![deny(warnings)]` (or `RUSTFLAGS="-D warnings"`) in the main crate to enforce a zero-warning policy during this task.
- Search for and list all items flagged in the task description using IDE tooling and `rg`/`grep` to confirm there are no remaining internal or external references.
- For any public APIs, config options, or plugin hooks that *might* be used externally, confirm deprecation plans or add a minimal shim instead of hard deletion if needed.

2. **Remove unused error variants**
- In the central error enums, delete the listed variants: `FileNotFound`, `PermissionDenied`, `FileRead`, `ConfigInvalid`, `MarkdownParse`, `MarkdownRender`, `SyntaxLoad`, `ThemeLoad`, `Initialization`.
- Remove associated `From`/`Into` impls, match arms, and error conversion helpers that only exist to support these variants.
- Run `cargo check` and update any remaining pattern matches that reference now-deleted variants (e.g., convert to wildcard arms or more specific matches as appropriate).

3. **Remove unused file operation helpers**
- In `files/operations.rs` (and related modules), delete unused functions: `read_file_contents`, `write_file_contents`, `validate_file_path`, `get_file_size`, `is_likely_text_file`, `ensure_parent_dir` when they are not part of the active file-open/save flows.
- Inline or replace any remaining internal uses with the current file open/save APIs.
- Re-run `cargo check` and ensure no callers remain; update imports and module exports accordingly.

4. **Remove unused config-related functions and types**
- In config modules, delete unused functions: `load_config_strict`, `load_or_create_config`, `config_exists`, `delete_config`, and any unused `ValidationError` type and settings validation methods.
- Remove any references from tests, documentation comments, and example code.
- If any of these are part of a public config API, either fully remove them and update docs, or mark the remaining minimal surface as the supported path.

5. **Remove obsolete editor line number structs**
- Locate and delete `LineNumberGutter` and `LineNumberGutterOutput` structs that were superseded by the current inline implementation.
- Replace any remaining type references with the new inline line-number rendering logic or associated helper types.
- Ensure editor modules compile and that line numbers still render correctly using the current implementation.

6. **Remove unused theme utilities and structs**
- In `theme/colors.rs`, `theme/mod.rs`, or related theme modules, delete unused color constants and functions: `blend_colors`, `darken`, `lighten`, `with_alpha`, `contrast_ratio`, and unused composite types such as `ColorPalette`, `ThemeFonts`, and `ThemeSpacing` when not required by the active `Theme` struct.
- Confirm that the `Theme` struct and any active palettes used by the UI or editor remain intact.
- Update any documentation that referenced the removed theme utilities so it reflects only the supported theme customization API.

7. **Remove unused markdown parser functions**
- In `markdown/parser.rs` and related files, delete unused helpers like `render_to_html`, `render_to_html_with_options`, and any `MarkdownDocument` inspection methods that are not used by the current rendering/WYSIWYG pipeline.
- Ensure that the remaining public API cleanly wraps comrak for the needed features only.
- Adjust docs and code comments so they no longer mention removed helpers or legacy rendering paths.

8. **Remove unused editable widget types and helpers**
- In UI/editor widget modules, remove unused structs: `EditableHeading`, `EditableParagraph`, `EditableList`, `ListItem`, and any associated formatting/helper functions that are not part of the active editing UI.
- Clean up module exports, imports, and any obsolete traits/impls that only served these widgets.
- If some styling or behavior logic is reused by current widgets, extract and retain only the minimal shared pieces and remove the rest.

9. **Remove unused syntax highlighter methods**
- Identify methods on syntax highlighter types that are never called (e.g., alternate render modes, debug helpers, legacy APIs).
- Delete these methods and any associated configuration flags or enums that support them.
- Ensure that the remaining syntax highlighting path used by the editor builds and behaves the same.

10. **Remove unused AppState methods**
- In the `AppState` implementation, remove unused methods: `toggle_settings`, `toggle_find_replace`, `set_status`, `clear_status`, `clear_toast` if there are no active UI bindings.
- Remove any now-dead fields, message variants, or UI actions that existed only to support these methods.
- Confirm that any status or toast behavior still present in the UI uses the remaining supported API.

11. **Trim unused Tab and UiState fields**
- In the `Tab` struct, delete unused fields `undo_stack` and `redo_stack`, along with any helper methods that manipulate them.
- In the `UiState` (or equivalent) struct, remove fields: `show_settings`, `show_file_dialog`, `show_save_as_dialog`, `show_find_replace`, `search_query`, `replace_text`, `status_message` where they are truly unused.
- Update struct initializers, builders, and serialization/deserialization code to match the new shape.
- Fix any remaining references in layout or state management code to avoid compilation errors.

12. **Remove unused PendingAction variants**
- In the `PendingAction` enum, delete unused variants: `CloseAllTabs`, `OpenFile`, `NewDocument` if they no longer participate in the app’s action handling.
- Update all pattern matches over `PendingAction` to remove or adjust arms referencing these variants.
- Confirm that the remaining action handling flow still compiles and that there are no unreachable pattern warnings.

13. **Documentation alignment**
- Search the documentation (README, user guide, API docs, inline rustdoc) for references to removed functions, types, fields, and variants.
- Update the docs to:
  - Describe the current, minimal public API for errors, config, theming, markdown parsing, editor widgets, and app state.
  - Remove or rewrite any examples that call deleted functions or rely on removed fields.
- Regenerate docs (`cargo doc`) and resolve any documentation warnings.

14. **Final cleanup and style pass**
- Run `cargo fmt` on the entire workspace.
- Run `cargo clippy` and address new lints introduced by this cleanup, adding `#[allow]` only where strongly justified.
- Run `cargo check` with warnings-as-errors and ensure **zero warnings** across all crates before considering the task complete.
tasks.testStrategy:
1. **Compilation and linting**
- Run `cargo check` (with `RUSTFLAGS="-D warnings"` or `#![deny(warnings)]`) and verify that the entire workspace builds successfully with zero warnings.
- Run `cargo clippy --all-targets --all-features` and confirm no new lints were introduced, or that any remaining lints have explicit, documented `allow` annotations.

2. **Automated tests and regression checks**
- Run the full test suite (`cargo test --all`) and ensure all tests pass after the deletions.
- If integration or UI tests exist for file open/save, tab management, line numbers, markdown parsing, and theming, verify they still pass without modification.

3. **Feature smoke testing**
- Manually verify core editor flows still work:
  - Open existing files via the native dialog.
  - Create new documents, edit them, and save/save-as to disk.
  - Switch between tabs and confirm content, dirty state, and window title behavior remain correct.
  - Confirm line numbers render correctly and any configured toggles still operate as expected.
  - Verify syntax highlighting, markdown rendering, and theming behave the same as before cleanup.

4. **Documentation validation**
- Build documentation with `cargo doc` and confirm there are no broken links or references to removed functions, types, fields, or enum variants.
- Spot-check key sections (errors, config, theming, markdown parser, editor widgets, app state) to ensure examples compile in doctests (if enabled) and reflect the updated API surface.

tasks.title: Replace Menu Bar with Ribbon UI
tasks.description: Replace the traditional egui menu bar with a modern ribbon-style interface featuring icon-based controls organized into logical groups, while preserving all existing keyboard shortcuts and ensuring smooth integration with the custom title bar and tab bar.
tasks.details:
**Implementation in `ui/ribbon.rs` and `ui/layout.rs`:**

1. **Remove existing menu bar:**
   - Locate and remove `egui::menu::bar()` calls from the main UI layout (likely in `ui/layout.rs` or `main.rs`).
   - Update layout to free up space previously occupied by the menu bar.

2. **Create Ribbon struct:**
   ```rust
   #[derive(Clone, Copy)]
   pub enum RibbonTab { File, Edit, View, Tools, Settings }
   
   pub struct Ribbon {
       active_tab: RibbonTab,
       collapsed: bool,
   }
   ```

3. **Implement ribbon widget with groups:**
   - Create `impl Ribbon` with `ui()` method that renders below custom title bar.
   - Use `ui.horizontal()` with `ui.separator()` between groups.
   - **File group:** New(📄), Open(📂), Save(💾), Save As(💾+)
   - **Edit group:** Undo(↶), Redo(↷), Cut(✂), Copy(📋), Paste(📋+)
   - **View group:** Raw/Rendered(👁), Line Numbers(📏)
   - **Tools group:** Find/Replace(🔍)
   - **Settings group:** Gear(⚙️)
   - Edit/Undo/Redo/Find/Replace buttons may initially be stubbed or disabled until their underlying functionality is implemented by their own tasks; the ribbon UI itself must not depend on those tasks being complete.

4. **Icon buttons with tooltips and shortcuts:**
   ```rust
   if ui.button(egui::Button::image(icon_new).frame(false))
       .on_hover_text("New (Ctrl+N)").clicked() {
       // Trigger new_file()
   }
   ```

5. **Collapsible behavior:**
   - Add collapse/expand toggle button at left of ribbon.
   - Use `ui.set_min_height()` to animate height between collapsed (icon-only) and expanded states.

6. **Wire actions to existing handlers where available:**
   - File-related ribbon buttons should call the existing file operation handlers already present in the app (e.g., the ones used by current keyboard shortcuts or menu commands), but this task no longer depends on specific file-op tasks by ID.
   - Undo/Redo and Find/Replace buttons should be wired up once their respective functionality exists, but their presence in the ribbon must not block this task; they can temporarily no-op or be visually disabled if needed.
   - View-related toggles (raw/rendered, line numbers) should integrate with the current editor/view state mechanisms without requiring explicit dependency tasks.
   - Settings (⚙️) should open whatever configuration or preferences UI currently exists in the app.

7. **Keyboard shortcut preservation:**
   - Ensure `ctx.input_mut(|i| i.raw.captured = true)` doesn't block Ctrl+S etc.
   - Test all shortcuts work through ribbon buttons and continue to work via the existing global shortcut system.

8. **Update layout flow:** Custom Title Bar → Ribbon → Tab Bar → Editor → Status Bar.

9. **Theme integration:** Use the existing Theme or styling configuration struct in the project (replacing any hard-coded colors) so the ribbon visually matches the rest of the UI.

10. **Responsive design:** Ribbon should adapt to window resize, wrapping groups if needed.
tasks.testStrategy:
1. **Visual verification:** Launch app, confirm menu bar removed, ribbon appears below title bar with 5 icon groups and separators.
2. **Button functionality:** Click each ribbon button (New/Open/Save/etc.) and verify correct actions trigger where functionality already exists (new tab opens, file dialogs appear, etc.); buttons for not-yet-implemented features should at minimum give no errors and present sensible disabled or placeholder behavior.
3. **Tooltip display:** Hover each icon, confirm tooltips show with keyboard shortcuts.
4. **Collapse/expand:** Toggle collapse button, verify ribbon height changes, icons remain accessible.
5. **Keyboard shortcuts:** Test Ctrl+N/O/S/Z/Y/F and any other existing shortcuts, confirm they work independently of ribbon state.
6. **View toggles:** Toggle Raw/Rendered and Line Numbers via ribbon, verify editor updates according to the current view/line-number implementation.
7. **Layout integrity:** Resize window, switch tabs, confirm ribbon stays positioned correctly above tab bar.
8. **Theme consistency:** Switch between available themes or appearance settings, verify ribbon colors and styling adapt properly.
9. **Integration test:** Complete a representative workflow (e.g., New→Edit→Save→Find) using ribbon controls where functionality exists, confirming no regressions in existing behavior.
10. **Regression:** Confirm status bar, undo/redo behavior, file operations, and other core features remain unchanged aside from their new ribbon entry points where hooked up.

tasks.title: Implement Settings Panel Modal with Live-Preview Editor Preferences
tasks.description: Create a settings modal or side panel accessible from the ribbon and keyboard that manages appearance, editor, and file-related options with immediate application and persistence to the config system.
tasks.details:
Implement a centralized settings UI and wiring that leverages existing AppState, ThemeManager, and config persistence.

1. **Overall structure and entry points**
- Add a `SettingsPanel`/`SettingsModal` component in `ui/settings.rs` (or similar) responsible purely for rendering and editing settings data.
- Extend `AppState` to track whether the settings UI is open, and whether it is shown as a modal dialog or side panel (enum like `SettingsViewMode::{Modal, SidePanel}` if needed).
- In `ui/ribbon.rs` (Task 41), add a **gear icon** in the `Settings` ribbon tab and wire it to toggle `AppState.show_settings = true`.
- In `ui/shortcuts.rs`, register **Ctrl+,** as a global shortcut that toggles the same flag, ensuring it works regardless of which tab or widget is focused.

2. **Settings data model**
- Reuse or extend the settings structure already stored in `AppState` and persisted via the existing configuration system.
- Ensure the structure contains at least:
  - `appearance.theme: ThemeChoice` (enum: `Light`, `Dark`, `System`).
  - `appearance.accent_color: Color` (e.g., RGBA or hex) with a sensible default (current light blue).
  - `editor.font_size: f32` or `u16`.
  - `editor.word_wrap: bool`.
  - `editor.show_line_numbers: bool` (must be wired to existing line number behavior).
  - `editor.tab_size: u8` or `usize`.
  - `files.default_save_location: Option<PathBuf>`.
  - `files.recent_files_count: u32`.
  - `files.auto_save_interval_ms: u32` or a typed duration.
- Define a `Settings::defaults()` function that returns a consistent set of default values used for first-run and for “Reset to Defaults”.

3. **UI layout and interaction design**
- Use a **clean, organized layout** in egui:
  - Left side: vertical list of sections: **Appearance**, **Editor**, **Files**.
  - Right side: section-specific controls.
- **Appearance** section:
  - Radio buttons or combo box for **Theme** (Light/Dark/System) bound to `settings.appearance.theme`.
  - Color picker (e.g., `egui::color_picker::color_edit_button_srgba`) for **Accent color** bound to `settings.appearance.accent_color`.
  - Optionally show a small preview region with headings, links, and buttons using the accent color.
- **Editor** section:
  - Slider for **Font size** within a reasonable range (e.g., 10–32) bound to `settings.editor.font_size`.
  - Toggle (checkbox) for **Word wrap**.
  - Toggle for **Show line numbers`, wired to the existing line number toggle.
  - Numeric input or slider for **Tab size** (e.g., 2–8 spaces).
- **Files** section:
  - Button and label for **Default save location**: pressing the button opens a native folder selection dialog (reuse the file dialog infrastructure already available in the project where possible) and stores the path in `settings.files.default_save_location`.
  - Spinbox or slider for **Recent files count** with validation (e.g., 0–20).
  - Spinbox or slider for **Auto-save interval** in seconds or minutes; convert to internal duration in the config.
- Add bottom-row buttons:
  - **Reset to Defaults**: resets the in-memory settings to `Settings::defaults()` and triggers persistence and live application.
  - **Close**: hides the settings UI (ESC key should also close when modal).

4. **Live application and theme integration**
- On any control change, immediately:
  - Update the `AppState.settings` field.
  - Call a central `AppState::apply_settings(&mut self)` (to be implemented) to propagate effects:
    - Notify **ThemeManager** to apply new `theme` and `accent_color` globally, including:
      - Links.
      - Headings.
      - Accent UI elements.
      - Selection highlights in the editor and other relevant widgets.
    - Update editor font size, word wrap, and tab size for all open tabs, or at least the active tab; repaint the editor widget.
    - Update line number visibility using the existing mechanism.
    - Trigger layout refresh for widgets that depend on theme or settings (status bar, title bar, ribbon, etc.).
- Ensure live changes do **not** require app restart; use egui’s immediate mode so all style updates are visible in the next frame.

5. **Config persistence and reset behavior**
- On every confirmed change (e.g., when a widget value actually changed), call the existing `save_config(&settings)` function from the configuration layer, ideally via a debounced layer to avoid excessive disk writes (simple approach: mark `settings_dirty` and flush at the end of the frame).
- Confirm that `AppState` initialization loads the settings at startup and uses them to initialize ThemeManager and editor configuration so settings **persist between sessions**.
- “Reset to Defaults” must:
  - Replace current settings with `Settings::defaults()`.
  - Apply live using `AppState::apply_settings`.
  - Persist the new default configuration.

6. **Window modality and persistence**
- Implement the settings UI as either:
  - An `egui::Window` with `modal`-like behavior (centered, with backdrop via custom drawing if needed), or
  - A side panel (`egui::SidePanel::right`) flagged as “Settings”.
- Ensure the **window open/closed state** is stored in `AppState` so it is consistent while the app runs; if “persist window between sessions” is required literally, add a simple `settings_ui_open_on_start: bool` to the config and restore it at startup.
- Make sure the settings dialog respects minimum sizes and scales with DPI so content is readable on all platforms.

7. **Integration with other UI components**
- Verify that:
  - Status bar picks up changes that affect any displayed info (e.g., theme name, encoding theme colors, etc.).
  - Ribbon (Task 41) uses accent color for highlighted buttons, active tab, and hover states where appropriate.
  - Editor applies font, tab size, word wrap, and selection color using ThemeManager’s accent color fields.
- Keep concerns separated: Settings UI should call into `AppState`/ThemeManager rather than manipulating low-level widgets directly.

8. **Error handling and UX robustness**
- Use the central error handling for failures such as saving config or opening directory dialogs; show non-intrusive error notifications in the UI if config cannot be saved.
- Validate user inputs (e.g., non-negative intervals, sensible ranges). Clamp values and show hints if out-of-range.
- Ensure keyboard navigation works: tab between controls, ESC to close, Enter does not accidentally reset.

9. **Documentation and code organization**
- Document all new settings fields and default values in code comments and any existing configuration docs.
- Keep settings-related logic in well-named modules (`state/settings.rs`, `ui/settings.rs`) for discoverability and future extension.
tasks.testStrategy:
1. **Unit tests (settings model and persistence)**
- Test `Settings::defaults()` returns the expected values for theme, accent color, editor, and file options.
- Test serialization/deserialization of the settings struct to ensure it round-trips correctly through the config persistence layer.
- Test `AppState::apply_settings` updates internal fields (theme, editor config flags) correctly when individual options are changed.

2. **Config integration tests**
- Start the app with no existing config; verify defaults are used and rendered correctly in the settings panel.
- Change multiple settings (theme, accent color, font size, word wrap, line numbers, recent files count, auto-save interval), close the app, restart, and verify all settings are restored from config.
- Use a deliberately corrupted config file and confirm that the app falls back to defaults but still allows editing and saving new settings.

3. **UI interaction tests (manual / exploratory)**
- Open the settings UI from the ribbon gear icon; verify it appears as designed and does not overlap critical UI.
- Trigger Ctrl+, from various contexts (editor focused, status bar, ribbon) and verify it consistently opens/closes the settings UI.
- Toggle each setting control and verify immediate visual feedback:
  - Theme changes alter overall app colors instantly.
  - Accent color changes update links, headings, accent buttons, and selection highlight in the editor without restart.
  - Font size, word wrap, and tab size visibly affect the editor content.
  - Show line numbers toggle shows/hides line numbers correctly.
  - Changing default save location opens a folder dialog and stores a new path.
  - Adjusting recent files count and auto-save interval updates associated behavior or at least internal state.
- Click “Reset to Defaults” and verify all values in the dialog return to default state, the UI updates immediately, and the next restart uses defaults.

4. **Behavior and persistence verification**
- With settings panel open, switch tabs, open/close files, and confirm no layout glitches or panics occur.
- Check that opening settings multiple times in one session maintains state only while open, and that any new changes are still persisted.
- If implementing persistence of the settings window state across sessions, open settings, restart app, and verify the window open/closed state is restored.

5. **Cross-component integration checks**
- Verify status bar still updates correctly after theme and font size changes.
- Verify the ribbon uses the current accent color for active/hover states and remains legible under light and dark themes.
- On each supported platform (Windows, macOS, Linux), confirm that the settings window renders correctly, keyboard shortcuts work, and config files are updated on disk as settings change.

6. **Error handling tests**
- Simulate a failure to write the config file (e.g., by making the directory read-only) and verify errors are handled via the central error module without crashing the app, and the user receives a clear, non-blocking notification.
- Enter out-of-range values (font size, tab size, auto-save interval) and verify they are clamped or rejected gracefully with no panics.

tasks.title: Implement Find and Replace Functionality
tasks.description: Add comprehensive find and replace functionality to the text editor with keyboard shortcuts, floating UI panel, match highlighting, and search options, accessible via Ctrl+F/H and ribbon toolbar.

tasks.title: Make Rendered Code Blocks Editable with Language Selection
tasks.description: Enable WYSIWYG-style editing of markdown code blocks in rendered mode, including in-place text editing, language selection, syntax re-highlighting, and synchronization back to the underlying markdown source.
tasks.details:
Implement an **EditableCodeBlock** widget in `markdown/elements.rs` (or the appropriate rendered-mode module) that operates on the parsed markdown AST nodes representing fenced and indented code blocks.

Core behavior and rendering
- Reuse the existing comrak-based AST and syntect integration to identify and render code blocks with syntax highlighting in rendered mode (fenced ``` and indented code blocks).
- In **view mode** (not actively editing), render code blocks as a non-editable, syntax-highlighted region using the existing syntect pipeline, including the existing **Copy** button functionality (ensure it still copies the full code content and remains clickable).
- In **edit mode**, replace the rendered code block with an egui `TextEdit` configured with:
  - Monospace font from the current `Theme` (e.g., via `theme/editor_font_mono` or equivalent).
  - Multi-line input, soft wrapping that matches preview behavior, and scrollbars as needed.
  - No syntax highlighting inside the `TextEdit` itself (plain text) to keep complexity manageable.

Edit mode lifecycle
- When the user **clicks anywhere on a code block** in rendered mode, switch that specific block into edit mode (similar to how Task 21 handles headings/paragraphs/lists):
  - Track edit state per-code-block, e.g., via an `is_editing: bool` or an editing ID in the WYSIWYG state.
  - Optionally support keyboard focus navigation (e.g., Tab/Shift+Tab to exit, but at minimum handle mouse click entry).
- On **blur/exit** from the `TextEdit` (losing focus, clicking outside, or pressing an explicit "Done" button if you add one), exit edit mode, re-run syntax highlighting, and re-render the block in view mode.
- Ensure that exiting edit mode always attempts to synchronize changes back to the markdown source (see below), even if there were no changes.

Language preservation and selection
- For fenced code blocks, preserve and expose the language info string (e.g., ```rust, ```python) by:
  - Reading the existing language identifier from the AST node and storing it in the widget state.
  - Maintaining this language when switching between view/edit modes.
- For indented code blocks (no explicit language), treat them as having a special language of **"plain text"** initially.
- In edit mode, render a **language selector dropdown** above or beside the `TextEdit`:
  - Supported languages (values and labels) should include at least: `rust, python, javascript, typescript, go, java, c, cpp, csharp, html, css, json, yaml, toml, markdown, bash, sql, plain text`.
  - Changing the selection updates the AST node's language info (for fenced code blocks) or converts the block into a fenced code block if necessary:
    - If an indented block’s language is changed from "plain text" to something else, convert it to a fenced block with that language.
    - If "plain text" is selected, you may keep it as fenced with an empty or `text` language, or convert to indented; choose one consistent representation and document it in comments.

Markdown synchronization
- Ensure that **all edits** in the `TextEdit` are reflected back into the underlying markdown source string as the **single source of truth**, consistent with Task 21 and Task 24:
  - Modify the AST node’s text content as the user edits (at least on blur/exit; optionally on each frame/change for live sync).
  - When the user exits edit mode, trigger markdown regeneration/serialization so that the raw markdown string:
    - Contains updated code block content.
    - Preserves or updates the language annotation (```lang) as selected.
    - Uses consistent fenced/indented style decisions defined above.
- For fenced blocks:
  - Ensure opening fence includes language when not "plain text".
  - Ensure closing fence is preserved and properly aligned.
- For indented blocks:
  - Maintain the required indentation level if you keep them as indented blocks, or ensure they are consistently converted to fenced blocks.

Syntax re-highlighting
- After exiting edit mode and updating the AST/markdown source, **re-run syntect-based syntax highlighting** for the updated content:
  - Ensure the language passed to syntect comes from the selected language dropdown (or default/plain text if none).
  - Handle cases where syntect does not have a highlighter for the chosen language by falling back to a generic or plain-text style.

Copy button behavior
- In view mode, keep the existing **Copy** button (or implement one if not present) on the code block:
  - The button should copy the **current code content** exactly as stored in the AST (not stale text from a previous render) so users can trust it reflects latest edits.
  - Ensure that clicking the copy button does **not** inadvertently enter edit mode or steal keyboard focus from elsewhere; make the click targets disambiguated.

Integration and architecture
- Integrate **EditableCodeBlock** alongside other WYSIWYG widgets (headings, paragraphs, lists, tables) using the same AST-driven architecture introduced in Tasks 19, 21, and 24:
  - Dispatch on the AST node type (code block / code fence) to instantiate this widget.
  - Store any per-block UI state (editing, language selection, cursor state if needed) in a suitable per-tab or per-document state structure.
- Ensure that the undo/redo system (Task 40) can eventually track edits made inside code blocks by:
  - Routing text changes back through the central content-update mechanism used by other widgets (e.g., modifications reflect as edits on the Tab’s `content` field).
  - Avoid maintaining a separate, unsynchronized buffer that bypasses the main text document.

Edge cases and UX considerations
- Support empty code blocks (no content) and allow creating and editing them without panics.
- Handle large code blocks efficiently; avoid per-character re-parsing of the whole document on every keystroke if performance becomes an issue (e.g., debounce updates, but still guarantee sync on blur).
- Clearly distinguish focus and edit state visually (e.g., subtle border or background change around the `TextEdit` and dropdown in edit mode).
- Ensure that switching between Raw and Rendered modes (Task 22) exits code-block edit mode gracefully and leaves the markdown content consistent.
- Consider keyboard shortcuts (optional, nice-to-have):
  - Esc to exit edit mode without changing language.
  - Ctrl+Enter to exit and commit changes.

Document decisions in code comments where behavior is non-obvious (e.g., how indented blocks are converted, how "plain text" is mapped to syntax highlighting).
tasks.testStrategy:
Manual and automated tests:

Rendered/edit behavior
1. In Rendered mode, open a document with multiple fenced code blocks (different languages) and indented code blocks.
2. Click a fenced code block: verify it switches to edit mode with a monospace `TextEdit` and visible language dropdown showing the current language.
3. Type inside the `TextEdit`, click outside the block: verify edit mode exits, syntax highlighting refreshes, and the displayed code matches the edits.
4. Switch to Raw mode and confirm the markdown shows a correctly formed fenced code block with updated content and language annotation.

Language selection and markdown sync
5. While editing a fenced block, change the language in the dropdown (e.g., from rust to python), exit edit mode, and verify:
   - Syntax highlighting uses the new language’s rules.
   - The fenced code block’s language info (```python) is correctly updated in the raw markdown.
6. For an indented code block, enter edit mode, change the language from "plain text" to `javascript`, exit edit mode, and verify:
   - The block is represented according to the chosen strategy (e.g., as a fenced block with ```javascript in markdown).
   - Syntax highlighting is applied using the selected language.
7. Change the language back to "plain text" and confirm behavior is consistent and markdown remains valid.

Copy button behavior
8. In view mode, click the Copy button on a code block, paste into an external editor, and confirm the copied text matches the latest edited content exactly.
9. Verify that clicking the Copy button does not enter edit mode or interfere with other UI interaction.

Round-trip and integration
10. Perform a series of edits across multiple code blocks (edit text, change languages, switch between Raw/Rendered modes repeatedly), then save and reload the file. Confirm that:
    - All code blocks display as expected with correct content and languages.
    - No structural corruption of markdown occurs (no missing fences, broken indentation, or malformed blocks).
11. Ensure other WYSIWYG widgets (headings, lists, tables) still function and that editing them does not affect code-block behavior.

Edge cases
12. Test empty code blocks: create an empty fenced block, edit it, add content, and verify serialization and highlighting work.
13. Test very large code blocks for reasonable performance (no noticeable freezes while editing, syntax highlighting still updates on blur).
14. Rapidly toggle between editing different code blocks and between Raw/Rendered view; confirm no panics, crashes, or desynchronized states.

(If available) Automated tests
15. Add unit tests for code-block markdown serialization/deserialization to ensure content and language info round-trip correctly between AST and markdown.
16. Add integration tests (if framework permits) to simulate enters/exits of edit mode and assert on resulting markdown and widget state.

tasks.title: Smart Link Handling in Rendered WYSIWYG View
tasks.description: Implement a simple hover-based link editing system in rendered (WYSIWYG) mode that allows viewing and editing link text/URL via a small popup, plus opening and copying the URL, while keeping the link itself non-clickable and synchronizing changes back to the markdown source.
tasks.details:
Implementation location and architecture
- Implement a dedicated **RenderedLinkWidget** (and possibly small helper structs) in `markdown/elements.rs`, integrated into the existing Comrak-based AST rendering pipeline used for rendered mode.
- Operate directly on the parsed markdown AST **link nodes** so that edits are propagated back to the underlying markdown source, similar to the EditableTable and EditableCodeBlock patterns.
- Reuse the existing view-mode toggle and rendered-mode infrastructure so that this behavior is **only active in Rendered mode** (not Raw text mode).
- Limit scope to **standard web links** (http/https) for now; treat other schemes and relative paths as plain text without special behavior.

Core behaviors (simplified hover menu)
1. Hover detection and settings icon
- In rendered mode, detect when the pointer is **hovering over a link** node.
- When hovered, render a small **settings/config icon button** (e.g., gear or pencil) adjacent to the link text (positioned to avoid overlapping other content as much as possible).
- The link text itself should still be rendered with basic link styling (e.g., theme link color and optional underline) but must **not be clickable**.

2. Popup/menu for editing
- Clicking the settings icon opens a small **popup/menu** near the link that contains:
  - An editable field for the **display text**.
  - An editable field for the **URL**.
  - An **"Open Link"** button that opens the current URL in the system browser using the existing `open` crate (only if it looks like an http/https URL; otherwise disable or no-op with graceful handling).
  - A **"Copy URL"** button that copies the current URL to the clipboard using the existing clipboard abstraction (if available in the app), or a stub/placeholder hook if clipboard support is centralized elsewhere.
  - A **"Done"** button that closes the popup and commits edits.
- The popup should capture focus while open and close when:
  - The user clicks **Done**, or
  - The user clicks outside the popup (if consistent with existing UI patterns).

3. Non-clickable link text
- The **link text must not trigger navigation** when clicked; navigation is only performed via the popup's **Open Link** button.
- Normal text selection behavior around links should be preserved as much as egui allows, but **inline editing directly in the rendered text is out of scope**.

4. Sync to markdown
- When the user edits the display text or URL in the popup and clicks **Done**:
  - Update the corresponding Comrak AST link node (label and destination).
  - Trigger regeneration of the underlying markdown source so the document contains updated `[text](url)` syntax.
- Ensure the edit → sync workflow is **reliable and deterministic**:
  - Changes should not be lost when switching between Raw and Rendered modes.
  - AST-to-markdown regeneration should be limited to the necessary scope or use the existing whole-document regeneration path, consistent with how other editable widgets work.

5. State management and integration
- Add minimal per-link UI state to track:
  - Whether the link is currently hovered (to show the settings icon).
  - Whether the popup is open for a given link.
  - Temporary text/URL field contents while the popup is open, before committing.
- Key state by a **stable identifier** for each link node (e.g., node pointer, byte range, or another existing node ID scheme) so it survives minor re-renders without leaking memory.
- Integrate with existing error handling patterns when `open` fails or clipboard operations are unavailable, surfacing them in a non-blocking way if such hooks already exist.

Out of scope / removed complexity
- No **Ctrl+Click** or double-click navigation.
- No **inline text editing** of link text in-place among other text.
- No **URL validation** or warning icons.
- No **relative file navigation** or special handling for local markdown paths.
- No **image link** previews or special image handling.
- No **Ctrl+K** or other keyboard shortcut for creating links.
- No special handling for non-http/https schemes beyond treating them as plain text URLs in the popup.

Non-goals and constraints
- Do not alter Raw mode editing semantics.
- Avoid introducing new global shortcuts; interaction is via hover, icon click, and the popup controls only.
- Keep the widget self-contained and simple, focusing on **basic link editing and navigation via the popup** rather than full WYSIWYG link behavior.
tasks.testStrategy:
Manual tests (focus on simplified behavior)

Rendered link interaction
1. Open a markdown document in **Rendered mode** with several http/https links.
2. Hover over each link and verify:
   - A small settings/config icon appears near the link while hovered.
   - The link text is visually styled as a link (color/underline as per theme) but clicking the text itself does **not** open the link.

Popup and editing
3. Click the settings icon and verify a popup appears containing:
   - An editable **Display text** field.
   - An editable **URL** field.
   - **Open Link**, **Copy URL**, and **Done** buttons.
4. Edit the display text and URL, click **Done**, and confirm:
   - The rendered link text updates to the new display text.
   - The markdown source now contains the updated `[text](url)` syntax at the correct location.
5. Close the popup by clicking **Done** and (if implemented) by clicking outside; verify it no longer intercepts input.

Open and copy behavior
6. With a valid http/https URL in the popup, click **Open Link** and confirm the URL opens in the system browser (e.g., via dev logs or visual confirmation).
7. Click **Copy URL** and verify the URL appears on the system clipboard (e.g., paste into another application), or that the app's clipboard diagnostics/logging confirm the copy.

Mode switching and persistence
8. After editing one or more links via the popup, switch between Raw and Rendered modes several times and confirm that:
   - The edited link text and URLs are preserved.
   - No crashes or desynchronization occur.

Regression and integration
9. Open documents containing links mixed with other rendered widgets (e.g., tables or editable code blocks) and verify that:
   - Hover icons and popups render in the correct positions.
   - Interactions with links do not interfere with other widgets.
10. Verify that non-http/https links behave safely (e.g., still editable in the popup but Open Link is disabled or handled gracefully, per implementation choice).

tasks.title: Fix Bold and Italic Rendering in WYSIWYG Markdown Editor
tasks.description: Audit and correct how bold, italic, and nested emphasis markdown is parsed and rendered in the WYSIWYG (Rendered) mode so that visual styling matches the underlying markdown and persists across view mode switches.
tasks.details:
Implementation steps:

1. **Audit current behavior in `markdown/editor.rs`**
- Locate the `MarkdownEditor` WYSIWYG rendering pipeline and identify where Comrak’s AST nodes are converted into egui widgets.
- Enumerate how inline nodes (text, links, emphasis, strong, code, etc.) are currently handled and note any missing or incorrect handling for `Strong` and `Emphasis` nodes.

2. **Ensure Comrak AST emphasis nodes are surfaced correctly**
- Inspect the types returned from the Comrak integration in `markdown/parser.rs` (from Task 19) and confirm how `NodeValue::Strong` and `NodeValue::Emph` are represented.
- If the inline rendering utility currently flattens or drops these nodes, refactor it to preserve an inline tree where emphasis/strong nodes wrap their children rather than being merged into plain text.
- Add helper functions (e.g., `render_inline_node(...)`) that can apply styles recursively based on node type, ensuring nested `Emph`+`Strong` combinations (for ***bold italic***) are maintained.

3. **Apply correct egui `RichText` styling**
- When building labels or other text widgets, use `egui::RichText` to apply formatting: `.strong()`/`.underline()` or explicit text styles as appropriate.[2][3]
- For bold: map both `**text**` and `__text__` (Comrak `Strong` node) to `RichText::new(text).strong()` or an equivalent bold style.
- For italic: map both `*text*` and `_text_` (Comrak `Emph` node) to `RichText::new(text).italics()` (or the appropriate API available in the project’s current egui version; if no direct italics API exists, map to an italic `TextStyle` or custom font/italics style).
- For underline variants (if supported by current markdown rules): ensure that representations meant to be underlined are mapped to `RichText::new(text).underline()`.[2]
- For nested formatting (***bold italic***), compose formatting by applying multiple style transformations on the same `RichText` instance (e.g., `.strong().italics()`), or by building a `LayoutJob` when you need multiple styled segments in one label.[1][2][6]

4. **Handle mixed-content and multi-span text**
- For inline sequences that contain both formatted and unformatted segments, build either:
  - A series of egui widgets (e.g., multiple `ui.label(RichText::...)` calls on the same line), or
  - A single `LayoutJob` with multiple sections, each configured from the corresponding AST span.
- Ensure whitespace, punctuation, and line breaks are preserved when reconstructing from the AST.

5. **Maintain round-trip behavior with Raw/Rendered modes**
- Confirm that WYSIWYG edits do not strip or alter emphasis markers in the underlying markdown string produced by `MarkdownEditor` (Task 20).
- Review how edits made in rendered widgets map back to markdown and ensure emphasis and strong markers (`*`, `_`) are preserved or updated correctly when text is edited inside formatted segments.
- Verify that switching between Raw and Rendered modes (Task 22, Task 26, Task 36) does not cause loss of styling: the same markdown should reparse to the same AST and render with identical bold/italic styling.

6. **Edge cases and nested formatting**
- Add internal examples and manual test data for:
  - Simple bold (`**bold**`, `__bold__`).
  - Simple italic (`*italic*`, `_italic_`).
  - Nested and combined emphasis (e.g., `***bold italic***`, `__*mixed*__`, `*__mixed__*`).
  - Emphasis inside links or list items.
  - Text spanning multiple lines or paragraphs with emphasis at boundaries.
- Confirm that emphasis does not bleed into adjacent text segments when markers are closed.

7. **Documentation updates**
- Update the existing WYSIWYG documentation to describe:
  - Which markdown emphasis syntaxes are supported (`*`, `_`, `**`, `__`, and combinations).
  - Any known limitations or behaviors with nested emphasis.
  - How bold/italic appear visually in the rendered view, including screenshots or short examples if the documentation format allows.
- Note any changes to internal APIs (e.g., new helper functions for inline rendering) in the developer documentation or code comments.

8. **Refactoring and cleanup**
- If old code paths for emphasis/strong handling are now obsolete, remove or refactor them for clarity, but coordinate with the broader dead-code cleanup (Task 39) to avoid conflicting changes.
- Ensure the updated code follows existing style and error-handling patterns used in `markdown/editor.rs` and related modules.
tasks.testStrategy:
1. **Unit and integration tests for AST-to-egui mapping**
- Add or extend tests that feed markdown strings into the Comrak-based parser and then through the inline rendering functions, asserting that:
  - `**bold**` and `__bold__` produce `RichText` (or layout sections) with bold styling only.
  - `*italic*` and `_italic_` produce `RichText` with italic styling only.
  - `***bold italic***` and nested combinations (e.g., `__*nested*__`) result in text segments styled with both bold and italic.
- Where direct inspection of `RichText` internals is difficult, test via helper APIs or by exercising the same code in a small test UI harness that exposes applied styles.

2. **Manual testing in the application UI**
- Create a markdown document with sections covering:
  - Simple bold, simple italic, and underline-like emphasis variants.
  - Mixed plain and formatted text on the same line.
  - Nested emphasis and emphasis within lists and links.
- Open this document in Raw mode, then switch to Rendered (WYSIWYG) mode and visually confirm formatting is correct for all cases.
- Edit text inside bold and italic spans in Rendered mode and switch back to Raw:
  - Verify that markdown markers (`*`, `_`, `**`, `__`) are preserved/updated correctly and no extra markers are introduced.

3. **View mode persistence tests**
- With a document containing a variety of emphasis styles:
  - Switch between Raw and Rendered modes multiple times using both the UI toggle and the keyboard shortcut.
  - Close and reopen the document (and, if applicable, restart the app) to ensure the saved view mode preference still restores correctly and that emphasis is still rendered as before.

4. **Regression tests and cross-checks**
- Confirm that non-emphasis markdown (headings, lists, code blocks, links, code spans, etc.) still render and edit correctly after the changes.
- Run the full test suite and `cargo check`/`cargo clippy` to ensure no new warnings or regressions are introduced.
- Perform a quick visual regression check for dark and light themes (Task 29) to confirm RichText styling (bold, italic, underline) remains readable across themes.

tasks.title: Improve WYSIWYG Markdown Editor Widget Interactions
tasks.description: Enhance the MarkdownEditor WYSIWYG mode so that text, headings, and list interactions behave like a word processor while keeping the markdown AST and source text in sync.
tasks.details:
Implement improvements in `markdown/editor.rs` (and related markdown element modules) to make rendered-mode editing feel natural and consistent:

1. **Review current MarkdownEditor behavior**
- Inspect how `MarkdownEditor` currently renders paragraphs, headings, and lists from the comrak AST.
- Identify the existing mapping between egui widgets and markdown nodes (e.g., paragraphs vs list items vs headings).
- Document how key presses (Enter, Backspace, Tab, Shift+Tab) are currently handled, if at all, in rendered mode.

2. **Text/paragraph editing (Enter in text blocks)**
- For editable paragraph widgets (plain text blocks), add key handling so pressing Enter splits the current paragraph at the cursor position.
- Implement a function that, given a paragraph node and cursor offset, returns two paragraph nodes and updates the AST accordingly.
- Ensure the AST update triggers regeneration of the markdown source string and re-render in the same frame or next frame.

3. **List editing basics (Enter to create new list item)**
- For list item widgets (ordered and unordered), intercept Enter key presses when the cursor is not at the beginning of an empty item.
- On Enter inside a list item:
  - Split the underlying list item text at the cursor into two list items.
  - Insert the new list item in the list node immediately after the current one, preserving list metadata (ordered/unordered, start index).
- Ensure bullet/number formatting is preserved in the regenerated markdown.

4. **List exit behavior (Enter on empty list item)**
- Detect when the user presses Enter on a list item whose text is empty (after trimming whitespace).
- On this condition:
  - Remove the empty list item from the list.
  - Insert a new paragraph node directly after the list node in the AST.
  - Place the caret at the start of the new paragraph in the UI.
- Handle edge cases where the empty item is the only list item (the entire list becomes a paragraph following previous content).

5. **Heading editing (Enter after heading)**
- For heading widgets (H1–H6), intercept Enter key presses.
- On Enter:
  - Do not create another heading node; instead, insert a new paragraph node immediately after the heading node in the AST.
  - Ensure caret focus moves into the new paragraph.
- Support Enter even if the cursor is midway through heading text, with semantics:
  - Option A (simpler): Always treat Enter as “new paragraph after heading” and keep heading unchanged.
  - Option B (if feasible): Split heading text at cursor, leaving text before cursor as heading and text after cursor as first text in new paragraph.
- Choose and document one behavior and keep it consistent.

6. **Backspace at start of list item**
- When Backspace is pressed at position 0 in a list item:
  - If there is a previous sibling list item:
    - Merge this item into the previous one: append current text to previous item’s text with a space, and remove current list item node.
    - Place caret at the correct merged position.
  - If there is no previous list item but there is a previous block node (e.g., paragraph or heading):
    - Convert the current list item into a paragraph (or merge into the previous paragraph) according to a clear rule:
      - Simpler rule: replace list item node with a paragraph node containing the same text.
- Ensure markdown regeneration reflects list item removal/merge correctly.

7. **Tab / Shift+Tab for list indent/outdent (sublists)**
- In list item context, handle Tab and Shift+Tab as structural operations rather than literal tab characters.
- **Indent (Tab):**
  - If there is a previous sibling list item:
    - Transform the current item into a child of the previous list item, creating or using a nested list node under that previous item.
    - Respect list type (ordered/ unordered), typically using the same list type as the parent list.
- **Outdent (Shift+Tab):
  - If the current list item is nested inside a list which itself is inside a parent list item:
    - Promote the item one level up: remove it from the nested list and insert it as a sibling list item after the parent list item.
    - If the nested list becomes empty, remove that nested list node from the AST.
- Carefully update AST so nesting structure remains valid and markdown serialization produces correct nested list syntax.

8. **AST and markdown synchronization**
- Centralize operations that modify structure (split paragraph, split list item, insert paragraph after list, indent/outdent) in helper functions that operate on the comrak AST.
- After every structural edit:
  - Re-serialize the AST back into markdown.
  - Update the MarkdownEditor’s source string.
  - Re-parse if necessary to keep the rendered view and markdown source in sync.
- Avoid infinite loops by ensuring that programmatic updates to the source don’t re-trigger the same edit handlers.

9. **Cursor and selection management**
- Extend the existing integration with AppState/editor widget so that after a structural change, the new cursor position is mapped back into the appropriate widget (paragraph, heading, or list item) and character index.
- Provide a small utility to map (node id, text offset) <-> absolute text offset or internal representation the editor uses, so cursor movement feels continuous across splits/merges.

10. **Nested list behavior**
- Build test examples of nested ordered and unordered lists (multi-level bullets and mix of ordered/unordered).
- Verify that Enter, Backspace, Tab, and Shift+Tab:
  - Preserve correct nesting levels.
  - Do not accidentally change list type unless explicitly required.
  - Do not corrupt the markdown (e.g., mismatched indentation or numbering).

11. **Edge cases and robustness**
- Consider:
  - Editing at start/end of headings and paragraphs (Enter/Backspace at boundaries).
  - Converting a one-item list into paragraph and back.
  - Undo/redo interactions using whatever mechanism exists in the main editor; ensure structural edits produce sensible undo steps.
- Add inline documentation and comments explaining the chosen editing rules, particularly where Markdown semantics and word-processor behavior differ.

12. **DeveloperNotes**
- Document the new keyboard interaction model in a markdown file (e.g., `docs/wysiwyg_interactions.md`) describing behaviors for Enter, Backspace, Tab, Shift+Tab in different node types.
- Include examples of markdown before and after each operation for future maintenance and regression testing.
tasks.testStrategy:
1. **Unit-level AST operation tests**
- Create focused unit tests for helper functions that modify the AST:
  - Splitting paragraphs on Enter at various positions (start, middle, end).
  - Splitting list items and inserting a new item.
  - Enter on empty list item converting to paragraph after list.
  - Heading Enter creating a paragraph after the heading.
  - Backspace at start of list item: merging with previous item vs converting to paragraph.
  - Indent (Tab) and outdent (Shift+Tab) of list items, including creation and removal of nested lists.
- For each test, assert:
  - The AST structure matches expectations (correct node types and hierarchy).
  - The serialized markdown string is exactly as expected.

2. **Integration tests for markdown sync**
- Using `MarkdownEditor` in rendered mode, feed in markdown samples:
  - Simple paragraphs and headings.
  - Flat unordered and ordered lists.
  - Deeply nested lists (3+ levels, both ordered and unordered combinations).
- Simulate key events (Enter, Backspace, Tab, Shift+Tab) at controlled cursor positions and verify:
  - The returned markdown from `MarkdownEditor` updates correctly and remains valid.
  - Round-tripping (serialize then parse again) preserves structure.

3. **Scenario-based interaction tests**
- Scenario: type a heading, press Enter, continue typing a paragraph; verify markdown uses `#` heading followed by a blank line and paragraph text.
- Scenario: type a bullet list of three items, pressing Enter at the end of each item; verify markdown shows three list items.
- Scenario: press Enter on an empty list item; verify that list ends and a new paragraph starts in both UI and markdown.
- Scenario: inside a nested list, use Tab and Shift+Tab repeatedly to change levels; verify nesting and bullet/number formatting in markdown and visual nesting in UI.
- Scenario: use Backspace at the start of a list item multiple times to merge with previous items or convert to paragraph; confirm behavior matches spec and markdown remains coherent.

4. **Nested lists regression tests**
- Create a set of sample docs with complex nesting (mixed ordered/unordered, headings, and paragraphs between lists).
- Perform combinations of Enter, Backspace, Tab, and Shift+Tab, then:
  - Ensure no panics or crashes.
  - Ensure markdown parses back into a structurally equivalent AST.

5. **Manual UX validation**
- Run the app and use the WYSIWYG mode as a typical user would:
  - Type and edit text as in a word processor, including quickly pressing Enter/Backspace/Tab in lists.
- Verify that interactions feel natural and predictable, and that switching between raw markdown and rendered modes does not introduce unexpected formatting changes.

6. **Regression and linting**
- Run the full test suite and `cargo check` to ensure no compilation issues.
- Optionally run `cargo clippy` to ensure no new obvious issues were introduced in the editor code paths.

tasks.title: Add Markdown Formatting Toolbar and Keyboard Shortcuts
tasks.description: Implement a markdown formatting toolbar integrated into the Ribbon UI and add keyboard shortcuts that apply common markdown operations in both raw and rendered editor modes, keeping toolbar state synchronized with the current selection.
tasks.details:
Implementation in `markdown/editor.rs`, `ui/ribbon.rs`, and `ui/shortcuts.rs` (plus any small helper modules as needed):

1. **Define formatting command model**
- Introduce an enum like `MarkdownFormatCommand` (e.g., `Bold`, `Italic`, `Link`, `Image`, `InlineCode`, `CodeBlock`, `Heading(u8)`, `BulletList`, `NumberedList`, `Blockquote`).
- Add a central function (e.g., `apply_markdown_command`) that takes the current editor mode (raw vs rendered), selection/cursor info, and command, and returns an updated document plus new selection/cursor state.
- Keep the low‑level text/AST transformation helpers in a separate module (e.g., `markdown/formatting.rs`) to keep UI code thin and testable.

2. **Raw mode formatting operations**
- For **inline syntax** (bold, italic, inline code):
  - Implement helpers that wrap the selected text with the appropriate delimiters (`**`, `*`, `` ` ``), or insert the delimiters at the cursor when there is no selection.
  - Implement toggle behavior: if the selection or surrounding text is already wrapped with the same syntax, remove the wrapping instead of adding another layer.
- For **links and images**:
  - On `Ctrl+K`, if text is selected, wrap it as `[selected](url)` and place the cursor with `url` selected; if no selection, insert `[text](url)` with `text` and/or `url` selected for quick replacement.
  - On `Ctrl+Shift+K`, insert `![alt](url)` and select `alt` or `url` as appropriate.
  - Implement simple heuristics to detect an existing markdown link/image around the selection and toggle/remove the full construct when already formatted.
- For **block structures**:
  - `Ctrl+Shift+C`: insert or toggle fenced code block around the current line or selection using triple backticks, preserving existing content and language tag if present.
  - Headings `Ctrl+1`…`Ctrl+6`: ensure the current line starts with the right count of `#` followed by a space; replace existing heading markers rather than stacking multiple `#` groups.
  - Bullet list `Ctrl+Shift+B`: toggle `- ` (or `* `) at the start of each selected line; when already a bullet, remove the marker.
  - Numbered list `Ctrl+Shift+N`: toggle `1. `, `2. `, etc., for each selected line; when already numbered, remove the markers.
  - Blockquote `Ctrl+Q`: toggle `> ` at the start of each selected line.
- Ensure all raw‑mode helpers operate on a plain string plus selection range and return: edited text, new selection range, and any scroll or cursor hints needed by the UI.

3. **Rendered (WYSIWYG) mode formatting operations**
- Build on the existing AST handling from Task 20 and interaction improvements from Task 47:
  - For inline formatting, locate the AST node(s) intersecting the caret/selection and wrap/unwrap them in appropriate inline nodes (strong/emphasis/code). Re‑serialize back to markdown using the existing AST → markdown path.
  - For headings, transform the current block node between paragraph and heading levels 1–6 while preserving inline children.
  - For lists and blockquotes, convert the current block node(s) to list items / ordered list / unordered list / blockquote nodes (and back) while preserving text.
  - For code block commands, convert selected blocks into fenced code block nodes or revert them to paragraphs.
  - For links and images, create or remove AST link/image nodes around the selected inline text and sync these changes back into the source markdown string.
- Ensure that rendered‑mode formatting always:
  - Updates the underlying markdown source string.
  - Triggers a reparse of the AST.
  - Leaves the caret/selection in a sensible location (e.g., on link URL or alt text where appropriate).

4. **Keyboard shortcut integration**
- Extend the global shortcut handling in `ui/shortcuts.rs` to map the following combinations to `MarkdownFormatCommand`s when the markdown editor has focus:
  - `Ctrl+B`: Bold
  - `Ctrl+I`: Italic
  - `Ctrl+K`: Insert/edit link
  - `Ctrl+Shift+K`: Insert image
  - `Ctrl+`` (Ctrl+backtick): Inline code
  - `Ctrl+Shift+C`: Code block
  - `Ctrl+1`..`Ctrl+6`: Heading levels 1–6
  - `Ctrl+Shift+B`: Bullet list
  - `Ctrl+Shift+N`: Numbered list
  - `Ctrl+Q`: Blockquote
- Ensure existing global shortcuts for files/tabs remain unaffected (Tasks 11 and 17 already define patterns to follow): route editor‑scoped shortcuts to the active editor instance instead of treating them as global when an editor is focused.
- Within `MarkdownEditor`, expose methods to apply a `MarkdownFormatCommand` using the current mode and selection, and call into the shared formatting helpers.

5. **Ribbon toolbar UI integration**
- In `ui/ribbon.rs`, under the appropriate Ribbon tab (likely **Edit**), add a **Markdown** or **Formatting** group with buttons for:
  - Bold, Italic, Inline Code, Code Block
  - Link, Image
  - Headings (either a dropdown or separate buttons for H1–H6)
  - Bullet List, Numbered List, Blockquote
- Each button should:
  - Dispatch the corresponding `MarkdownFormatCommand` to the active `MarkdownEditor`.
  - Support tooltips showing the markdown syntax and associated keyboard shortcut.
- Ensure toolbar buttons are disabled or visually inactive when no markdown editor is active.

6. **Formatting state reflection in toolbar**
- In `MarkdownEditor`, compute a lightweight **formatting state** for the current caret/selection (e.g., is inside strong/emphasis/inline‑code/link/list/blockquote; current heading level).
- Expose this state via an accessor or callback that the Ribbon can query for the active editor.
- In `ui/ribbon.rs`, use this state to:
  - Highlight the Bold/Italic/Inline Code/etc. buttons when the caret is inside corresponding formatting.
  - Indicate current heading level on the heading control (e.g., selected entry in dropdown or highlighted H‑button).
- Update the state whenever:
  - The caret or selection changes.
  - The mode (raw vs rendered) changes.
  - A formatting command is applied.

7. **Raw vs rendered mode coordination**
- Reuse the existing mode toggle logic from `MarkdownEditor` (Task 20) so that:
  - Toolbar buttons and shortcuts operate correctly in both modes by calling the same `MarkdownFormatCommand` API, with different underlying implementations (string vs AST).
  - After a command in rendered mode, the raw text view (when switched) reflects the new markdown syntax; after a command in raw mode, the rendered AST view updates on next render.
- Make mode‑specific corner cases explicit, e.g.:
  - When a selection spans multiple heterogeneous blocks in rendered mode, either apply formatting only to compatible blocks or gracefully decline the operation (e.g., no action, or limited transformation) while keeping state consistent.

8. **Performance and UX considerations**
- Ensure re‑parsing the markdown AST after formatting commands does not cause visible lag for typical document sizes; if necessary, limit reparses to when changes actually occur.
- Avoid surprising behaviors such as doubling delimiters, breaking links, or corrupting list structure. Prefer simple, predictable transformations.
- Keep all operations undo‑friendly by modifying the underlying text in a single coherent edit per command so the existing undo stack (if present) behaves sensibly.
tasks.testStrategy:
1. **Unit tests for raw‑mode formatting helpers**
- For each inline command (bold, italic, inline code):
  - Verify wrapping behavior with and without selection.
  - Verify toggle/removal when selection or surrounding text is already formatted.
  - Check that cursor/selection indices returned match expected positions.
- For links and images:
  - Selection becomes `[selection](url)` or `![selection](url)` with `url` selected.
  - No‑selection cases insert placeholder text and URL with correct selection.
  - Existing link/image around the selection is removed cleanly (whole construct removed, surrounding text intact).
- For block commands:
  - Code block: selection of multiple lines becomes fenced block, and a fenced block toggles back to plain lines.
  - Headings: lines are converted to H1–H6 and existing headings change level instead of stacking `#`.
  - Bullet and numbered lists: toggling adds/removes appropriate markers on multi‑line selections.
  - Blockquote: toggling adds/removes `> ` on each selected line.

2. **Unit tests for rendered‑mode AST operations**
- Given small AST fragments:
  - Applying bold/italic/inline code wraps nodes with appropriate inline wrappers and unwraps on toggle.
  - Applying heading changes the node type while preserving inline children.
  - Applying list and blockquote commands transforms paragraphs to the correct block containers and back.
  - Applying link/image creates and removes corresponding AST nodes correctly.
- After each operation, re‑serialize AST to markdown and assert expected markdown output.

3. **Integration tests for `MarkdownEditor` behavior**
- In raw mode:
  - Simulate text content and selection, call `MarkdownFormatCommand` through the editor, and assert editor text and selection match expected results.
- In rendered mode:
  - Provide markdown with paragraphs, headings, lists, links, images, code blocks.
  - Place caret programmatically inside a word or node, apply commands (bold, heading, list, blockquote, code block, link) and verify:
    - Returned markdown string is updated as expected.
    - Re‑rendered WYSIWYG view reflects the new formatting.

4. **Shortcut mapping tests**
- In `ui/shortcuts` tests or an integration harness:
  - Simulate the key combinations for each command while a markdown editor is focused and confirm the correct `MarkdownFormatCommand` is invoked.
  - Verify that file and tab shortcuts from Tasks 11 and 17 still work and are not intercepted by the editor when they should remain global.

5. **Ribbon toolbar tests**
- Instantiate the Ribbon with an active `MarkdownEditor`:
  - Click each toolbar button and assert the corresponding `MarkdownFormatCommand` is sent to the editor.
  - Verify tooltips show the right keyboard shortcut labels.
- With known markdown documents and specific caret positions:
  - Query formatting state from `MarkdownEditor` and confirm the Ribbon highlights the correct buttons (e.g., Bold when in strong text, correct heading level selected, list/blockquote state).
  - Move caret across differently formatted regions and verify the Ribbon updates its state on each movement.

6. **Manual exploratory testing**
- In raw mode:
  - Type sample markdown, then use toolbar buttons and keyboard shortcuts to apply/toggle all supported formats, including across multi‑line selections.
  - Confirm syntax is inserted/toggled correctly and that switching to rendered mode reflects the expected formatting.
- In rendered mode:
  - Use only mouse selection plus toolbar buttons for headings, lists, blockquotes, links, images, and code blocks; verify the markdown view (when toggled) shows the correct syntax.
  - Verify that the toolbar accurately reflects current formatting state as the caret moves through bold, italic, list items, headings, and plain text.
- Confirm undo/redo (if present) behaves sensibly after a sequence of formatting operations in both modes.

tasks.title: Implement Document Outline / Table of Contents Side Panel
tasks.description: Add a configurable side panel that displays a live-updating, clickable outline of document headings (H1–H6), usable in both raw and rendered markdown views, with keyboard toggle and basic summary stats.
tasks.details:
Implementation plan:

1. **Data model and heading extraction**
- Add a reusable heading extraction module, e.g. `editor/outline.rs`.
- For **raw mode**, parse the markdown source text to identify headings H1–H6:
  - Use the same parsing stack as the WYSIWYG markdown editor (comrak AST) to avoid duplicate parsing logic where possible.
  - Extract a flat list of heading entries: `{id, level, text, byte_offset_or_range, line, estimated_read_time_contribution}`.
  - Generate a stable `id` for each heading (e.g. index-based plus hash of text) to support selection and collapse state.
- For **rendered mode**, reuse the markdown AST already produced for WYSIWYG (Task 20/47) and walk the AST to collect headings with their levels and node references.
- Expose a function like `fn compute_outline(doc: &MarkdownDocument) -> Vec<OutlineItem>` where `OutlineItem` contains level, text, position, and children indices.

2. **Outline panel UI component**
- Create a new UI module, e.g. `ui/outline_panel.rs`, implementing an `OutlinePanel` egui widget.
- Layout:
  - Vertical panel that can be attached to left or right side of the editor area.
  - Top summary section showing **heading count** and **estimated read time** (e.g. based on total word count from headings+document or using existing stats module from Task 32).
  - Scrollable list area for the hierarchical outline.
- Render outline items as a tree:
  - Indent based on `level` (H1 root, H2 one indent, etc.).
  - Each row contains: optional expand/collapse icon, heading text (truncated/ellipsized if long), and possibly level indicator in subtle styling.
  - Maintain a `collapsed: HashSet<HeadingId>` (or map) in UI state to support collapsible sections.
- Highlight current section:
  - Track a `current_heading_id: Option<HeadingId>` in state.
  - Apply a distinct background or accent color from `Theme` (Task 27) to the active heading row.

3. **Panel behavior and interactions**
- **Toggling visibility**:
  - Add an "Outline" toggle button in the appropriate ribbon/toolbar group.
  - Integrate a global keyboard shortcut **Ctrl+Shift+O** in `ui/shortcuts.rs` (building on Task 11/17) that toggles the outline panel for the active tab.
- **Click-to-navigate**:
  - When a heading row is clicked, scroll the associated editor (raw or rendered) so that the heading is near the top of the viewport.
  - For raw mode, convert stored byte offset/line into a scroll offset on the `EditorWidget` (Task 7) and move the cursor into that line.
  - For rendered mode, use the AST node reference or a previously recorded layout position from the WYSIWYG editor to scroll the markdown editor to that section.
- **Collapsible sections**:
  - Clicking the expand/collapse icon next to a heading toggles visibility of its descendant headings.
  - Ensure collapse/expand operations are O(n) at worst and do not require re-parsing.
  - Preserve collapse state per document while the tab is open; optionally store minimal state if tabs are reused.
- **Panel side selection (left/right)**:
